corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: The advantages of the U-Net appear not to be specific to conditional GANs: when both U-Net and encoder-decoder are trained with an L1 loss, the U-Net again achieves the superior results.
Details of the architecture are provided in the supplemental materials online, with key features discussed below.

To explore the generality of conditional GANs, we test the method on a variety of tasks and datasets, including both graphics tasks, like photo generation, and vision tasks, like semantic segmentation: • Semantic labels↔photo, trained on the Cityscapes dataset . • Architectural labels→photo, trained on CMP Facades . • Map↔aerial photo, trained on data scraped from Google Maps. • BW→color photos, trained on . • Edges→photo, trained on data from and ; binary edges generated using the HED edge detector plus postprocessing. • Sketch→photo: tests edges→photo models on humandrawn sketches from . • Day→night, trained on . • Thermal→color photos, trained on data from . • Photo with missing pixels→inpainted photo, trained on Paris StreetView from .

In Figure , we investigate whether our cGANs actually achieve this effect on the Cityscapes dataset.
In each case we use the same architecture and objective, and simply train on different data.
Our goal in this paper is to develop a common framework for all these problems.
Our second contribution is to present a simple framework sufficient to achieve good results, and to analyze the effects of several important architectural choices.
Our second contribution is to present a simple framework sufficient to achieve good results, and to analyze the effects of several important architectural choices.

The generator G is trained to produce outputs that cannot be distinguished from "real" images by an adversarially trained discriminator, D, which is trained to do as well as possible at detecting the generator's "fakes".

To explore the generality of conditional GANs, we test the method on a variety of tasks and datasets, including both graphics tasks, like photo generation, and vision tasks, like semantic segmentation: • Semantic labels↔photo, trained on the Cityscapes dataset . • Architectural labels→photo, trained on CMP Facades . • Map↔aerial photo, trained on data scraped from Google Maps. • BW→color photos, trained on . • Edges→photo, trained on data from and ; binary edges generated using the HED edge detector plus postprocessing. • Sketch→photo: tests edges→photo models on humandrawn sketches from . • Day→night, trained on . • Thermal→color photos, trained on data from . • Photo with missing pixels→inpainted photo, trained on Paris StreetView from .
Using a 16×16 PatchGAN is sufficient to promote sharp outputs, and achieves good FCN-scores, but also leads to tiling artifacts.
The 70 × 70 PatchGAN alleviates these artifacts and achieves slightly better scores.
A U-Net architecture allows low-level information to shortcut across the network.
